originalTitle: This extravagant gaming laptop could ruin other screens for you
summary: My eyes have seen the PC gaming promised land, and it's a beautifully bright world without a shred of blurriness. It's warm, it looks lovely, and it's impeccably sharp. Also, it's expensive as hell. I've dipped my toe in this world by testing a pre-production version of the upcoming Asus ROG Strix Scar 18, which […]
content: My eyes have seen the PC gaming promised land, and it’s a beautifully bright world without a shred of blurriness. It’s warm, it looks lovely, and it’s impeccably sharp. Also, it’s expensive as hell. I’ve dipped my toe in this world by testing a pre-production version of the upcoming Asus ROG Strix Scar 18, which was recently announced ahead of Computex 2026. It’s a gigantic 18-inch gaming laptop that comes with a top-of-the-line 24-core Intel Core Ultra 9 290HX CPU and can be fully kitted out with an RTX 5090 Laptop GPU and 128GB of RAM. Asus sent me a model to test that’s maxed out on all specs except storage (it’s got “just” 4TB). And of course, the company isn’t announcing any pricing yet — not even for a base RTX 5080 model. We all know, thanks to RAMageddon, it’s going to be gut-punchingly expensive. But what makes the Strix Scar 18 especially unique is its screen: a 4K, 240Hz display with an anti-glare matte finish and a special feature called Extreme Low Motion Blur (ELMB). The Mini LED panel has over 2,000 dimming zones and up to 1,600 nits of peak brightness in HDR mode. It’s when you turn HDR mode off that the magic actually happens. This allows you to use ELMB and have all those dimming zones automatically split up the display into smaller horizontal bands of pixels, refreshing them row by row very quickly — kind of like a traditional CRT. [Image: How ELMB works compared to a traditional screen, according to Asus’ promotional video. https://platform.theverge.com/wp-content/uploads/sites/2/2026/05/ELMBhowitworks-gif.gif?quality=90&strip=all] The result is an amazingly crisp picture that shows almost no motion blur during fast action in games. And it achieves this without black frame insertion — a more common method to reduce motion blur that causes a flicker effect and can negatively affect screen brightness. And as you’d expect, this thing is an absolute beast when it comes to playing the most graphically demanding games. I loaded up Cyberpunk 2077 in 4K on Ultra settings with ray tracing turned on and DLSS set to Balanced, and it maintained a respectable (though beautiful) 45fps. That boosted to about 70fps with DLSS turned to Ultra Performance, but you could easily throttle down some other settings or engage frame generation for much higher frame rates. A less graphically demanding competitive shooter like Counter-Strike 2 could run at high settings and maintain 180 to 200fps. It’s the “frames win games” kind of titles like Counter-Strike and even MOBAs like League of Legends that the ELMB display complements the most. If you want to have the fastest twitch-level response to what you see onscreen, you want the clearest picture possible — and that means a fast-moving image free of motion blur. But even if you’re not an esports player with god-tier reflexes, it’s quite satisfying to play fast-action games with this level of sharpness and clarity. And when you don’t need it, you can switch off ELMB and turn on HDR to get eye-searing levels of brightness during more cinematic games. [Image: https://platform.theverge.com/wp-content/uploads/sites/2/2026/05/268564_Asus_ROG_Strix_Scar_18_ELMB_2026_handson_prepro_ADiBenedetto_ADiBenedetto_0002.jpg?quality=90&strip=all] [Image: https://platform.theverge.com/wp-content/uploads/sites/2/2026/05/268564_Asus_ROG_Strix_Scar_18_ELMB_2026_handson_prepro_ADiBenedetto_ADiBenedetto_0004.jpg?quality=90&strip=all] [Image: https://platform.theverge.com/wp-content/uploads/sites/2/2026/05/268564_Asus_ROG_Strix_Scar_18_ELMB_2026_handson_prepro_ADiBenedetto_ADiBenedetto_0005.jpg?quality=90&strip=all] [Image: https://platform.theverge.com/wp-content/uploads/sites/2/2026/05/268564_Asus_ROG_Strix_Scar_18_ELMB_2026_handson_prepro_ADiBenedetto_ADiBenedetto_0006.jpg?quality=90&strip=all] ELMB’s smoothness is very difficult to capture in photos or on video (though I tried my best in the images below). But here’s a simple, repeatable method you can follow along with. At the very least, you can see how your monitor or laptop display handles the same torture tests and compare it to what I describe while doing the same on the Strix Scar 18’s ELMB display.  (Disclaimer: These torture tests feature colorful fast-moving graphics. If you’re prone to motion sickness this could make you a little queasy. User discretion, yada yada. Be cool. Don’t sue us.) Here’s what you do: * Make sure your screen refresh rate is set to its highest setting. (If you’re on a laptop, plug in the power adapter to prevent any battery saving from slowing down your panel.) * Load up the Video Game Motion test on Blur Busters’ site (use this direct link). * Click the full-screen icon in the top-right corner of the auto-scrolling test window. The top half should be 240Hz and the bottom should be whatever your native refresh rate is set to. * Once the frame rate numbers turn green, the test has loaded and stabilized. * Now, look closely at the details of the characters scrolling across the frame. Can you follow the action across the screen and clearly read the character names in this scene from Dota 2? On a normal screen, the motion is a blurry mess. But on the Scar’s ELMB panel, it’s all crystal clear. If I track the action across the screen (before giving myself a headache with these fast-scrolling speeds) I can read names like Lion, Vengeful Spirit, and Dragon Knight with ease. I can also make out what level each hero is and exactly how many bars of health they’re down. If I flip off ELMB to use other features like HDR, things are still somewhat legible but noticeably blurrier, even with the screen’s 240Hz refresh rate. I can no longer make out hero names, health bars, and levels as easily. I have to really focus my eyes and rely on my perception to think, “Oh yeah, that looks like it says Sven.” And by the time I do, it’s off the screen. The action’s gone, and if this were a competitive online game that probably means my reaction time would be slightly slower too. [Image: https://platfo
chineseTitle: 这款奢侈的游戏笔记本电脑可能会毁掉你的其他屏幕
chineseSummary: 我亲眼目睹了电脑游戏的乐土，那是一个美丽明亮的世界，没有一丝模糊。它很温暖，看起来很可爱，而且非常锋利。而且，它非常昂贵。我通过测试即将推出的华硕 ROG Strix Scar 18 的预生产版本来涉足这个世界，它 [...]
chineseContent: 我亲眼目睹了电脑游戏的乐土，那是一个美丽明亮的世界，没有一丝模糊。它很温暖，看起来很可爱，而且非常锋利。而且，它非常昂贵。我通过测试即将推出的华硕 ROG Strix Scar 18 的预生产版本来涉足这个世界，该产品最近在 Computex 2026 之前发布。这是一款巨大的 18 英寸游戏笔记本电脑，配备顶级 24 核英特尔酷睿 Ultra 9 290HX CPU，可以完全配备 RTX 5090 笔记本电脑 GPU 和 128GB 内存。内存。华硕给我发了一个模型来测试，除了存储之外，它的所有规格都达到了极限（它“只有”4TB）。当然，该公司尚未宣布任何定价 - 甚至没有公布基本 RTX 5080 型号的定价。我们都知道，由于 RAMgeddon，它的价格将会非常昂贵。但 Strix Scar 18 的独特之处在于它的屏幕：4K、240Hz 显示屏，具有防眩光哑光饰面和称为“极低运动模糊”(ELMB) 的特殊功能。 Mini LED 面板拥有超过 2,000 个调光区域，在 HDR 模式下峰值亮度高达 1,600 尼特。当你关闭 HDR 模式时，奇迹就会真正发生。这允许您使用 ELMB 并让所有这些调光区域自动将显示器分割成更小的水平像素带，非常快速地逐行刷新它们 - 有点像传统的 CRT。 [图片：根据华硕的宣传视频，ELMB 与传统屏幕相比如何工作。 https://platform.theverge.com/wp-content/uploads/sites/2/2026/05/ELMBhowitworks-gif.gif?quality=90&strip=all] 结果是一张令人惊讶的清晰图片，在游戏快速动作期间几乎没有运动模糊。它无需黑帧插入即可实现这一目标，黑帧插入是减少导致闪烁的运动模糊的更常见方法。
whyItMatters: 这条信息属于 AI 与科技趋势信号，适合用于观察工具选型、产品机会、开发者关注点和技术路线变化。
